entry.title: Re: Recurring Payments from SAP to EC
entry.content:
Infotype 0014 corresponds to Recurring Pay and Recurring Deductions portlets in EC.

Data can be send from SAP HCM to EC via different ways: 

  • Standard interface package (infoporter) , see https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_SUCCESSFACTORS_EMPLOYEE_CENTRAL_INTEGRATION_TO_SAP_BUSINESS_SUITE/602c38f7400b4d2693602a178c2bf028?locale=en-US&state=PRODUCTION&version=2605
  • Custom build interface 
  • CSV import

It depends on the concrete use case and requirements, which of the above fits best. 

Interfaces into Recurring Pay should take into account the following in order to avoid unwanted data losses or inconsistencies: 

  • Basic pay (infotype 0008) and recurring pay (infotype 0014) are stored in the same Recurring Pay portlet in EC
  • Recurring pay can potentially be updated by different processes,e.g. manual updates in EC during hiring/promotion, annual compensation review from Compensation Management, automated pay-scale increase etc.  --> find out which process are relevant in your context and make sure that interfaces are designed accordingly

entry.title: Re: Portugal – Support for Original / Duplicate / Triplic...
entry.content:
Hello!

SAP provide a partial standard mechanism and supported extensibility paths that together can address the requirement.

SAP has a built-in Original/Duplicate watermark mechanism for billing document output, documented in KBA 3023958. This works as follows:

When a billing document is printed for the first time, the system passes a flag (IV_DUPLICATE_INDICATOR) into the Adobe Form at render time. On the first print this flag is blank, the form displays no watermark or displays "Original." On any subsequent reprint, the flag is set and the form displays "Duplicate."

This mechanism is already implemented in the standard form rendering pipeline. This is country-activated,  SAP only enables it for countries where it is legally required. Portugal is not currently in the list of activated countries. The standard Original/Duplicate distinction can be activated for Portugal by raising a SAP support message requesting that Portugal be added to the country activation table. This is a configuration-only change on SAP's side. Reference KBA 3023958 when opening the message.

To extend to triplicate and full reprint tracking, the standard framework is binary: it only distinguishes original from duplicate. To cover the full requirement including triplicate labeling and sequential reprint tracking, two additional components are needed, both within the supported Public Edition extensibility model:

Step 1.  Add a print counter field using BAdI SD_BIL_PRINT_STANDARD_HEAD

Using the Custom Fields and Logic app, create a custom field on the billing document (Business Context: Sales: Billing Document) to store a print counter. Implement the BAdI SD_BIL_PRINT_STANDARD_HEAD to increment this counter each time an output request is created for the document: 1 for the first print, 2 for the first reprint, 3 for the second reprint, and so on. This counter is then exposed to the Adobe Form through the OData data source. This approach is documented in KBA 2679030.

Step 2.  Drive the copy label in the Adobe Form using LiveCycle Designer scripting (the setting are documented in KBA 2984817). Open the billing document form template in Adobe LiveCycle Designer . Bind a text field to the counter value from the data source. Add a JavaScript or FormCalc script at form level,  for example:

if CopyNumber == 1 then "Original"

else if CopyNumber == 2 then "Duplicate"

else if CopyNumber >= 3 then "Triplicate"

For same run copies (all three copies printed simultaneously on first print). If your business process requires all three copies to be produced in a single print run at the time the invoice is first issued, rather than through sequential reprints,  this can be achieved within Adobe LiveCycle Designer by creating three separate master pages, each with its own label (Original, Duplicate, Triplicate), and assigning each master page to the corresponding copy number via the form's pagination settings. The constraint is that this only works when all copies are generated in one output run.

 

KBA 3023958  Watermark Duplicate/Original for Billing Document Output in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (standard mechanism, country activation, Customer Influence request)

KBA 3596806  Printing Multiple Copies of Billing Document in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Original, Duplicate, Triplicate  confirmed gap and paths)

KBA 2679030   Extensibility for Billing Document Printout in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (BAdI SD_BIL_PRINT_STANDARD_HEAD and Custom Fields)

KBA 2984817  Form Design in Adobe LiveCycle Designer (scripting, form templates, merge  the technical reference for form-level implementation)

entry.title: How to avoid 5-digit UoM limit using chained alternative units in S/4?
entry.content:
Hello experts,

I’m currently trying to better understand how Alternative Units of Measure (AUoM) work in SAP S/4HANA, especially in more complex scenarios.

From my understanding:

  • Each material has a Base Unit of Measure (BUoM)
  • Alternative units are defined with a conversion factor to the base unit
  • All quantities are internally converted to the base unit for stock management.

 In my case, I need to model large conversions, but I want to avoid exceeding SAP limits in numerator/denominator (max ~5 digits).

For example:

  • 1 PALLET = 1,000,000 PCS → ❌ not allowed directly
  • So I’m considering breaking into intermediate units:
    • 1 BOX = 1,000 PCS
    • 1 PALLET = 1,000 BOX

 

When defining unit of measure conversions, what is the correct process in SAP S/4HANA?
 
Thanks in advance.
 
Regards.

entry.title: Bonus Assignment Statements-Custom Fields
entry.content:
Hi Experts

I am facing a issue with outputting  custom fields created in Variable Pay module on the Bonus Assignment statement ( it is a custom XSLT statement).  These custom fields have been created at the "Business Goal" level using lookup table method as the source of information.

The following SAP defined steps have been followed:

  1. Import Var Pay relevant employee and other data from EC( Employee History)
  2. Set up Var Pay system in the Var Pay module
  3. Run Bonus calculation
  4. Generate Bonus Assignment statement

What is a puzzling behaviour is that when above mentioned steps are followed the "Bonus Assignment statement" does get generated but without any of the Custom fields.

In addition to the above mentioned, if I launch the worksheet (not desirable) and then generate the "Bonus Assignment statement" then these Custom fields do get reflected on the statement.

As there is bit of nebulousness in the standard SAP documentation on Bonus assignment statements, I am seeking your help to get to the desired state of getting these custom fields displayed on the "Bonus Assignment" statement without the unnecessary launch of any worksheets/forms.

Thanks

entry.title: Live Versions for Planning Models in SAP Analytics Cloud: Microsoft Azure Is Here!
entry.content:
Introduction

The live data access story for SAP Analytics Cloud planning models keeps getting stronger. When we introduced live versions in QRC4 2025, we opened the door for planning models to connect directly to live SQL sources without replication. Google BigQuery was the first supported source, followed by Snowflake in QRC1 2026. With this release, Microsoft Azure joins the portfolio — and the pattern should by now be a familiar one.

If you missed the earlier posts in this series, here is a quick recap: live versions allow a planning model in SAP Analytics Cloud to reference fact data directly from an external source at query time, without importing or replicating that data into SAC. The live data is read-only — you cannot write back to the source — but you can use it in calculations, data actions, and stories, and copy it into planning versions when needed. This applies to SAC models that are not deployed to SAP Datasphere. For seamless planning scenarios, live connectivity to SAP Datasphere is the way to go. Learn more:

  • Live Versions from an SQL Source for SAP Analytics Cloud Planning
  • SAP Analytics Cloud Planning with Live Snowflake Data
  • Unlocking the Next Chapter of Seamless Planning in SAP Business Data Cloud with Live Versions

With Microsoft Azure now supported, customers running their data on Azure can bring that data directly into their SAC planning models without any replication pipeline. Let's walk through how it works.

 

How Does It Work?

There are two ways to get started with live versions from Microsoft Azure: you can create a new model directly from your Azure data, or add a live version to an existing model. We will cover both approaches.

Creating a New Model from Azure Data

Alternatively, you can create a planning model directly from your Azure data source — a clean approach when starting from scratch. The video example is based on Snowflake. It is the same workflow, just choose Azure instead.

  • Go to the Modeler and select Create Model
  • Choose Create Model from Data
  • Select Microsoft Azure — you can filter the connection list by Data Storage "Cloud" to find it quickly
  • Choose your connection and select a view
  • The model is created along the structure of the source view, with a live connection established immediately — visible in the data preview

From here, you work with the model as you would with any other SAC planning model. You can remove dimensions and measures you do not need, define calculations directly on the live data, and use data actions to copy actuals from Azure into a plan version stored in SAC's own fact table.

Working with the model

 

Use live version data in planning

Adding a Live Version to an Existing Model

Open your model and navigate to the Data section in the model details. You will find a new entry called External Live Version Data Sources.

From there, the steps are straightforward. The video example is based on Google BigQuery. It is the same workflow, just choose Azure instead.

  • Select Connect External Data Source
  • Choose an existing target version or create a blank one
  • Select Microsoft Azure as the source system
  • Choose your connection
  • Select your data source from Azure
  • Map the data source columns to your model columns — not every source or target column needs to be mapped, measures can be NULL, and dimensions can receive a default member
  • Review any performance hints or master data mismatch notifications — missing dimension members can be added directly from this dialog
  • Start using the live data in calculations, data actions, and stories

Add a live version to an existing model

You can add multiple live sources to a single model if needed.

 

FAQ

Where can I use this feature?

This feature is available for SAP Analytics Cloud models in SAP Analytics Cloud tenants running on HANA Cloud that are not deployed to SAP Datasphere (i.e. not using seamless planning).

Can I use any source view from Azure?

Yes, with the caveat that not all data types are supported. Please refer to the product documentation for the full list of supported data types.

What can I do with live versions — and what are the limitations?

You can:

  • Display live version data in the model data foundation and in tables, charts, and other widgets in stories
  • Reference live version data in model and story calculations as well as data actions, including advanced formulas

You cannot:

  • Write back to the Azure source directly. To persist planned data, copy the live version data into a planning version via copy/paste in the table, data actions, or version management — the plan data is then stored in the SAC fact table

How do I know if data is live or replicated?

In the model data preview, live versions are highlighted and the data source is shown per version in the version dimension. In stories, live version data is locked for data entry and formatted distinctly. Unplannable data reasons provide accurate context.

Conclusion

With Microsoft Azure joining Google BigQuery and Snowflake as a supported live source, SAP Analytics Cloud continues to expand its openness to the data platforms customers already use. Whether you are running your fact data in Azure, Snowflake, or BigQuery, you can now bring it directly into your planning model without any replication overhead — and without giving up the full power of SAC planning, calculations, and stories.

This is part of SAP Analytics Cloud's broader alignment with SAP Business Data Cloud's ambition to be a truly open platform. Follow the Roadmap Explorer to stay up to date on further additions to the live connectivity portfolio. Live connectivity to Microsoft SharePoint Excel files and Microsoft SharePoint Lists is currently planned for QRC4.2026!

Have questions or feedback? Drop them in the comments below.

entry.title: Re: How to find authorization roles with an object and specific field values tied together?
entry.content: To get the assigned users together with your authorization field values have a look at joining tables AGR_USERS and AGR_1251 in the quick viewer (SQVI) Once you've got your result set, sorting on the field AGR_1251-AUTH "Authorization name in user master maintenance" will help you to get the required field combinations.

entry.title: Re: ECP and S/4 HANA Payroll _ Saudi BADI
entry.content:
Unfortunately, SAP does not provide a centralized repository for all available BAdIs applicable to Saudi Arabia localization. To identify relevant BAdIs, use the following approach:

Transaction SE18 Search

Search for BAdI definitions using these patterns:

  • Badi name: *SA* and search for "KSA" or "SAUDI", and *PAYSA*
  • Enhancement Spot: *SA_* or *PAYSA*

Alternative Resources

  • SAP Help Portal: https://help.sap.com (search for "PY-SA")
  • SAP Support Portal: https://me.sap.com/home (search within PY-SA component)

Finally, about DMEE, SAP does not provide a Saudi Arabia-specific WPS BAdI equivalent to PADOM_WPS_FIELDS or PADQA_WPS_FIELDS as you already know.

For KSA localization requirements, you could consider implementing a custom DMEE tree .

Best Regards

Giorgio

entry.title: Dynamic Criticality in SAP CAP Using Fiori Elements and Node.js
entry.content:
Introduction

While working on a CAP application recently, I came across a requirement where records needed to be highlighted based on their status. The business users wanted a quick way to identify whether an order was completed, still in progress, or blocked without having to read through each record individually.

My first thought was to store a criticality value directly in the database, but that didn't feel like the right approach because the value was purely UI-related and depended on the business status. After exploring a few options, I found that CAP and Fiori Elements provide a clean way to calculate criticality dynamically in the backend and display semantic colors automatically in the UI.

In this blog, I'll walk through the approach I used to achieve this using SAP CAP, Node.js, OData annotations, and Fiori Elements. By the end, you'll have a working example where criticality is calculated at runtime and reflected directly in your Fiori application.

Why Use Dynamic Criticality?

In many business applications, the visual status of a record depends on changing business conditions. For example, an order may move from "In Process" to "Completed", or an expense request may be "Approved" or "Rejected".

Storing criticality values in the database means the values must be maintained whenever the business status changes. By calculating criticality dynamically in the CAP service, the UI always reflects the latest business state while keeping the database model focused on business data.

Step 1: Create a CAP Project

mkdir criticality-demo 
cd criticality-demo 
cds init 
npm install 
npm install sqlite3

Step 2: Create the Data Model

Create the file db/schema.cds

namespace demo.criticality;

entity SalesOrders {
  key ID           : Integer;
      CustomerName : String(100);
      Amount       : Decimal(10,2);
      Currency     : String(3);
      Status       : String(30);
}

Step 3: Add Sample Data

          Create the file:  db/data/demo.criticality-SalesOrders.csv

ID;CustomerName;Amount;Currency;Status
1;ABC Corporation;1200.00;INR;Completed
2;Global Traders;5000.00;INR;In Process
3;Tech Solutions;800.00;INR;Blocked
4;Amazon;900.00;INR;In Process

Step 4: Create an OData Service

          Create the file srv/service.cds.

using demo.criticality as db from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
  entity SalesOrders as projection on db.SalesOrders;
}

Step 5: Configure Fiori Annotations

          Create the file app/annotations.cds.

using CatalogService as service from '../srv/service';

annotate service.SalesOrders with @(
  UI.LineItem : [
    {
      $Type : 'UI.DataField',
      Label : 'Customer',
      Value : CustomerName
    },
    {
      $Type : 'UI.DataField',
      Label : 'Amount',
      Value : Amount
    },
    {
      $Type : 'UI.DataField',
      Label : 'Status',
      Value : Status,
      Criticality : Criticality,
      CriticalityRepresentation : #WithIcon
    }
  ],

  UI.SelectionFields : [
    CustomerName,
    Status
  ]
);

          The most important configuration is:

Criticality : Criticality

This tells SAP Fiori to use the Criticality value exposed by the OData service to determine the color and icon displayed for the Status field.

Step 6: Create a Fiori Elements Application

Open the SAP Fiori Application Generator and select:

  • Template: List Report Object Page
  • Data Source: Use a Local CAP Project
  • Service: CatalogService
  • Entity: SalesOrders

Complete the wizard to generate the Fiori Elements application.

Step 7: Run the Application

Start the CAP application:

cds watch

Step 8: Make Criticality Dynamic Using Node.js

Since Criticality is calculated dynamically, we do not need to persist it in the database. Instead, we expose it as a computed field in the service projection and populate it at runtime based on the business status.

A useful side effect of this approach is that if the business decides to change the criticality rules in the future, only the CAP service logic needs to be updated, without requiring database changes or frontend modifications.

At this stage, we will update the service definition created in Step 4 to expose a computed Criticality field.

using demo.criticality as db from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
  entity SalesOrders as projection on db.SalesOrders {
    *,
    null as Criticality : Integer
  };
}

           Then Create srv/service.js:

module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function () {
  this.after('READ', 'SalesOrders', (orders) => {
    const list = Array.isArray(orders) ? orders : [orders];

    for (const order of list) {
      switch (order.Status) {
        case 'Completed':
          order.Criticality = 3;
          break;
        case 'In Process':
          order.Criticality = 2;
          break;
        case 'Blocked':
          order.Criticality = 1;
          break;
        default:
          order.Criticality = 0;
      }
    }
  });
});

With this implementation, criticality is calculated at runtime based on the business status rather than being stored in the database.

As shown below, Fiori automatically renders different semantic colors based on the Criticality value calculated in the CAP service. Completed orders appear in green, In Process orders appear in yellow, and Blocked orders appear in red.

Understanding Criticality Values

SAP Fiori uses the following standard criticality values:

Criticality Value Meaning Color Typical Usage

0NeutralGrayDefault or informational status
1NegativeRedError, Rejected, Blocked
2CriticalOrange/YellowWarning, Pending, In Process
3PositiveGreenSuccess, Approved, Completed

Conclusion

What I liked about this approach is that the database remains focused on business data while the CAP service handles the UI-specific logic. Instead of maintaining criticality values in a table, we can derive them dynamically from the status and expose them through the service whenever the data is requested.

One thing that took me some time to figure out was that the calculated field must also be exposed in the service projection. Without that step, the application receives the status value but not the criticality information needed for the semantic colors.

Although this example uses sales order statuses, the same pattern can be applied to approval workflows, expense reports, support tickets, or any scenario where users need a quick visual indication of the current state of a record. I hope this helps anyone looking to implement dynamic criticality in their own CAP applications.

entry.title: Temperature-Controlled Shipment Planning in SAP TM — A Complete Configuration Guide SAP Blogs | SAP
entry.content:
Business Scenario

A shipper receives a Sales Order containing multiple products with varying storage conditions:

Product Storage Condition Description

Product AABAmbient
Product BRFRefrigerated (2°C – 8°C)
Product CRTTemperature Controlled (15°C – 25°C)

The system must ensure these products are planned into separate Freight Units and that incompatible storage conditions are never consolidated onto the same transport unit.

Transactional Flow Overview

Here is the end-to-end transactional journey:

 

Step 1: Create a Sales Order with Mixed Storage Conditions

The process begins in SAP S/4HANA (or SAP ECC) where a Sales Order is created with multiple line items, each carrying products that have different storage conditions maintained in the Material Master (MRP2/Plant Data tab).

Key Master Data Prerequisites

  • Material Master → Plant Data / Storage 1 tab: Storage Condition field populated per material.
  • Storage conditions are configured as a custom condition type or use SAP standard condition codes depending on your system setup.

What Happens Here

When the Sales Order is saved and the TM integration is active, the order is forwarded to SAP TM as an Order-Based Transportation Requirement (OBTR) or Delivery-Based Transportation Requirement (DBTR), carrying the storage condition information at the item level.

Step 2: Freight Unit Creation Based on Storage Conditions

Once the transportation requirement arrives in SAP TM, the system applies the Freight Unit Building Rule (FUBR) to create Freight Units (FUs).

The FUBR is configured to split Freight Units by Storage Condition using the Incompatibility Setting. This means:

  • Product A (AB - Ambient) → Freight Unit 1
  • Product B (RF - Refrigerated) → Freight Unit 2
  • Product C (RT - Temperature Controlled) → Freight Unit 3

Each Freight Unit now carries a homogeneous set of products from a storage condition perspective, ready for compatible planning.

Configuration of the FUBR a Incompatibility Setting is assigned to FUBR with the same Logic as planning but the Incompatibility Area= Freight Unit Building and validity of the Incompatibility definition is 

 

 

Step 3: Planning of Freight Units Using Incompatibility Rules

During the planning phase, the SAP TM Optimizer or manual planning tools use the Incompatibility Setting assigned to the Planning Profile to ensure that Freight Units with incompatible storage conditions are never placed on the same Freight Order or Transport Unit.

This acts as a hard block — the system will not allow, even manually, the consolidation of incompatible storage conditions unless the block is explicitly overridden by an authorized user.

Created a Incompatibility Setting with Area as " Automatic Planning and Manual Planning and the Incompatibility definition with below Validity 

 

 

Important to note is the Condition type should be /SCMTMS/INC_FU with identical Values only activated.

entry.title: Auto-Populating Hiring Team Roles from Position Matrix Relationships
entry.content:
The Product Gap

Product Gap: There is currently no standard (out-of-the-box) option to map Position Matrix Relationships from SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central to SmartRecruiters Hiring Team roles. This limitation applies when a job requisition is created from a Position in SmartRecruiters — the matrix relationships (e.g., HR Manager, Dotted-Line Manager) defined on the source Position are not automatically resolved and assigned as Hiring Team members.

This feature is on the SAP product roadmap but is not yet generally available.

In SAP SuccessFactors, the Position Matrix Relationship is a powerful feature that allows organizations to define secondary or indirect reporting relationships alongside the primary line hierarchy. Common examples include HR Business Partners, Dotted-Line Managers, or Functional Leads assigned to a position. These relationships are defined at the Position level and, by extension, apply to the incumbent employee.

In SmartRecruiters, the Hiring Team on a job requisition controls who can view, manage, and collaborate on the requisition. Typical roles include Recruiter, Coordinator, and Hiring Manager. When a requisition is created from a Position in SuccessFactors, it is expected that the right stakeholders — as defined by matrix relationships — would be automatically added to the Hiring Team with the correct roles. This does not happen today via standard integration.

Solution Architecture

 

 

Component Role in the Solution

SmartRecruiters Webhook (job.created)Triggers the automation when a job is created from a Position
EC OData – Job / Position linkRetrieves the Position code tied to the new job
EC OData – PositionMatrixRelationshipReturns all matrix relationships defined on the Position
EC OData – EmpJobFinds the current incumbent on the related/matrix Position
SmartRecruiters Users APIResolves the SuccessFactors user to a SmartRecruiters user ID
SmartRecruiters Hiring Team APIUpserts the resolved user with the correct Hiring Team role
 
Note: The integration layer (webhook listener and orchestration logic) can be implemented on SAP BTP using Integration Suite, Cloud Integration, or a custom Node.js / Python function. The focus of this blog is on the API interactions rather than the middleware implementation.

Step-by-Step Implementation

 

Subscribe to the job.created Webhook in SmartRecruiters

Configure a webhook subscription in SmartRecruiters for the job.created event. Whenever a recruiter creates a new job requisition — particularly one initiated from a Position in SuccessFactors — SmartRecruiters fires this event with the new Job ID in the payload.

 

Figure 1: The job.created webhook payload containing the SmartRecruiters Job ID

From the payload, extract the Job ID (e.g., 2a9e277c-275e-42ad-9749-2137a3a2e9f8). This ID will be used throughout the subsequent steps.

 

Retrieve the Associated Position Code from SmartRecruiters / EC

Using the Job ID returned by the webhook, query the SmartRecruiters or Employee Central API to retrieve the Position code associated with the job. In the standard SuccessFactors–SmartRecruiters integration, the Position code is stored against the job requisition and can be retrieved via the SR Job details endpoint or the EC job requisition linkage.

The Position code is the key that unlocks the matrix relationship query in the next step. Example Position code: 3001511

 

Query Position Matrix Relationships in Employee Central

With the Position code, call the EC OData API endpoint for PositionMatrixRelationship, filtering by the Position code and expanding the related position navigation:

GET https://apisalesdemo8.successfactors.com/odata/v2/PositionMatrixRelationship
    ?$filter=Position_code eq '3001511'
    &$expand=relatedPositionNav

The response contains the matrix relationship type (e.g., hr manager) and the related Position code, which identifies the Position held by the matrix relationship incumbent.

 

 

Figure 2: EC OData API response for PositionMatrixRelationship — showing matrixRelationshipType: hr manager and the related Position

Note the matrixRelationshipType field — this can later be mapped to the corresponding Hiring Team role in SmartRecruiters (e.g., "hr manager" → RECRUITER or a custom role).

 

Identify the Incumbent on the Related Position

Once you have the related Position code from Step 3, query the EmpJob entity in EC to find the current incumbent (employee) holding that position:

GET https://apisalesdemo8.successfactors.com/odata/v2/EmpJob
    ?$filter=position eq '3000775'
    &$select=userId,userNav/displayName
    &$expand=userNav

The response provides the userId of the incumbent in Employee Central (e.g., Tworkshop) along with their display name.

Resolve the SmartRecruiters User ID

SmartRecruiters maintains its own internal user IDs that are different from SuccessFactors user IDs. To upsert a user to the Hiring Team, you need the SR-specific user ID. There are two approaches to resolve this mapping:

Option A — Query the SR Users API: Call the SmartRecruiters Users endpoint and match the employee by email address or name to retrieve their SR user ID.

GET https://api.smartrecruiters.com/users

 

Figure 3: SmartRecruiters Users API response — SR user ID: 6943a4116e5fce00ab01c71c

Option B — Maintain a Custom MDF in SuccessFactors: Run a daily synchronization job that queries the SR Users API and stores the SR user IDs in a custom Metadata Framework (MDF) object in BizX, keyed by the SuccessFactors userId. At runtime, a simple lookup on this MDF returns the SR ID without making an additional API call to SmartRecruiters, reducing latency.

Recommendation: Option B (custom MDF with daily sync) is preferred for production scenarios as it reduces runtime API dependencies and improves reliability. Option A is simpler to implement for proof-of-concept or low-volume scenarios.
 

Upsert the User to the SmartRecruiters Hiring Team

With the SR Job ID (from Step 1) and the SR User ID (from Step 5), make a POST request to the SmartRecruiters Hiring Team endpoint to add the user with the appropriate role:

POST https://api.smartrecruiters.com/jobs/{jobId}/hiring-team

Body:
{
  "id": "6943a4116e5fce00ab01c71c",
  "role": "RECRUITER"
}

The role value should be mapped from the matrixRelationshipType retrieved in Step 3. A suggested mapping is:

 

EC matrixRelationshipType SmartRecruiters Hiring Team Role
hr managerRECRUITER
dotted line managerCOORDINATOR
(custom)(custom mapping as per your configuration)

 

Figure 4: POST to the SmartRecruiters Hiring Team API — 200 OK confirms the user was successfully added

Result

After executing the upsert in Step 6, the SmartRecruiters Hiring Team section on the job requisition is automatically populated with the correct members derived from the Position Matrix Relationships in Employee Central.

In the example below, the hiring team has been updated with two members:

  • Kunal Hasija — Coordinator
  • Test Workshop — Recruiter (added via the API upsert)

 

Figure 5: SmartRecruiters Hiring Team before the upsert — only one member is present

 

Figure 6: SmartRecruiters Hiring Team after the upsert — the matrix relationship incumbent has been added as Recruiter

Outcome: The Hiring Team in SmartRecruiters is now automatically populated based on the Position Matrix Relationships defined in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central — without any manual intervention from the recruiter.

Key Considerations and Recommendations

  • Role Mapping: Define a clear and agreed mapping between EC matrix relationship types and SR Hiring Team roles before implementation. This mapping should be configurable, not hardcoded.
  • User ID Synchronization: Maintaining SR user IDs in a custom MDF in BizX reduces runtime latency and avoids failures caused by SR API rate limits.
  • Multiple Matrix Relationships: A single Position may have multiple matrix relationships. Ensure your implementation iterates over all returned relationships and calls the upsert for each resolved incumbent.
  • Error Handling: If a matrix relationship incumbent is not found in SmartRecruiters (e.g., they are an external contractor not in SR), implement graceful error handling and alerting rather than failing the entire process.
  • Webhook Reliability: Use retry logic and idempotent upsert calls to handle cases where the webhook fires multiple times for the same job creation event.
  • Future Native Support: This gap is on the SAP product roadmap. Monitor SAP release notes and the SAP SuccessFactors Community for updates. Once native support is delivered, the custom solution should be retired to reduce maintenance overhead.

Summary

The absence of a standard mapping between Position Matrix Relationships in SAP SuccessFactors and the SmartRecruiters Hiring Team is a recognized product gap that affects customers using the integrated SuccessFactors–SmartRecruiters talent acquisition setup. Until native support is delivered, the six-step custom solution described in this blog provides a reliable, event-driven workaround:

  1. Subscribe to the SmartRecruiters job.created webhook
  2. Retrieve the Position code associated with the new job
  3. Query EC PositionMatrixRelationship OData API
  4. Identify the incumbent via EC EmpJob OData API
  5. Resolve the SmartRecruiters User ID (via SR Users API or custom MDF)
  6. Upsert the user to the SmartRecruiters Hiring Team with the mapped role

 

Thanks

 

Kunal

Product Success

RIG APJ

entry.title: Re: Restart Failed E-Signatures Without Restarting the Entire Onboarding Process post 1H 2026 Releas
entry.content:
@Radhika_Windlass03 
Onboarding document flow step restart works in below cases:

  1.     If document flow status is completed or skipped, user can restart the document flow.
  2.     If document flow is triggered after release and is in status in-progress, user can restart the document flow.
  3.     If document flow was triggered before release and is still in-progress after release, user will not be able to restart this document flow. This document flow needs to be declined to regenerate the documents again.

Pre-requisites are mentioned here: Restart e-Signature Step for Document Flows

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 05 - Portable functions
Re: How to Implement SAP Business One projects successfully
entry.content:
Great breakdown on the implementation steps. Having run 20+ SMB go-lives in the Hyderabad market, I'd add one critical nuance: the go-live week is where Hyderabad SMBs lose the most money, and it's almost never a technical issue. It's a data readiness issue.

Three failure patterns I see repeatedly:

1. Master data validation is skipped under time pressure.

Teams migrate from Tally/legacy spreadsheets and the item master is "good enough" on cutover night. Three weeks later, inventory reports are useless because unit of measure conversions weren't standardized. The fix: run a parallel inventory count in the old system AND SAP B1 for 2 weeks before go-live. Any variance >2% stops go-live until resolved.

The deeper problem? Nobody has real-time visibility into what's actually diverging between systems during the parallel run. You're comparing spreadsheets manually — which defeats the purpose. We've started using AI-driven batch cost gap analysis during parallel runs to flag exactly which items, batches, and cost centers are out of sync — before cutover night, not after.

2. User training is treated as a checkbox.

SMB owners assume the consultant will "handle it." What actually happens: 3 PowerPoint slides, a rushed demo, and a team that can log in but can't process a goods receipt. The result: workarounds that corrupt data, followed by 6 months of cleanup.

Better model: identify 2–3 power users per department in week 1. Train them deeply. They become your go-live support army. And give them tools they can actually use — natural language queries against live SAP data ("show me all open purchase orders over 5 lakhs") reduce the training burden significantly because the interface matches how people already think.

3. No cutover rollback plan.

If something breaks on go-live night, what's the decision tree? Who decides to rollback vs. push through? This sounds obvious but almost no SMB implementation documents this. The result: 48 hours of chaos before the inevitable rollback — which costs more than if you'd planned for it.

The single best investment: A formal 3-day parallel run before go-live where the team processes every real transaction in BOTH systems simultaneously. Painful? Yes. Expensive? Yes. Cheaper than data recovery after a bad cutover? Far less.

Go-Live Readiness Checklist (what we use at TEKROI):

  • Parallel run confirmation — both systems running simultaneously for at least 5 business days
  • Real-time data validation — automated gap detection across inventory, cost centers, and batch records (not manual spreadsheet comparison)
  • Rollback decision criteria documented — who decides, under what conditions
  • Data freeze window — no master data changes 48 hours before cutover
  • Go-live support team identified — internal champion + consultant available for 72 hours straight
  • First-week close check — finance runs month-end in SAP B1 within 5 days
  • KPI dashboard live on Day 1 — cashflow forecast, inventory turnover, batch age visibility from the first hour of go-live (not "we'll set up reporting later")

Teams that skip this checklist average 3–4 weeks of post-go-live disruption. Teams that use it average 3–4 days.

For pharma companies in Patancheru and Genome Valley — add batch traceability validation to this checklist. If your FG batch genealogy chain isn't complete before go-live, your first USFDA or WHO-GMP audit will be expensive.

What specific module are you struggling with? Happy to go deeper.

— Venkata Siva Reddy Polu, TEKROI
SAP B1 implementation & AI operations intelligence for Hyderabad pharma and manufacturing SMBs

entry.title: Unable to Restrict Edit Functionality in Display Maintenance Order App
entry.content: We would like to restrict the Edit functionality in the Display Maintenance Order app. Currently, users can view and also edit maintenance orders, but the requirement is to provide display-only access. This indicates that an additional catalog, IAM app, or authorization is granting Edit access. We request help to identify the authorization responsible and guidance on how to restrict editing while keeping display access. I have prepared a Word document with attached screenshots showing what we are currently experiencing and what we desire.

entry.title: Enhancing Goods Receipt and Floor Stock with Custom Attributes for Enhanced Traceability
entry.content:
Introduction

In SAP Digital Manufacturing, Data Fields and Data Types can be used to enhance manufacturing processes by improving traceability, by collecting and storing custom attribute values during the manufacturing process. Once Data Fields and Data Types are created, they can be configured in Manage Materials app, to be collected during relevant production processes steps like component assembly or floor stock receipt, for example.

In addition to that, a little known custom field configuration is available in Order POD Goods Receipt plugin, to collect a custom attribute value during Goods Receipt posting.

In this blog, I will show in detail some very specific ways of using those custom attributes during Inventory ID handling and Goods Receipt posting, that can be very useful to enhance the overall traceability strategy.

Data to Collect on Floor Stock Receipt

This functionality is used to store additional information for inventory management purposes.

After 'Data to Collect on Floor Stock Receipt' mapping is done to a Data Type in Manage Materials app:

you can maintain values for the the Inventory ID data fields assigned to the data type specified for the material through the PATCH /inventories/{inventoryId} API:

or via Production Process:

* Before updating the Inventory ID, first you must read it as shown above, to retrieve modifiedDateTime value, which is a required input in order to update it.

Once Inventory ID data is updated, it looks like this:

You can also maintain these values by manually editing the Inventory ID itself, directly via Manage Floor Stock app.

Enrich Manage Floor Stock app to display Data Fields as Filters and Results

In Manage Business Settings app > Inventories section, you can adjust 'Display Data Fields' setting to include up to 4 Data Fields:

These Data Fields will then be added as options to Manage Floor Stock app filters and results.

To display them, in Manage Floor Stocks app, click 'Adapt Filters' and select the Data Field(s) to enable them as filters:

Click 'Table Settings' and select the Data Field(s), to add them as columns to the results table:

This will allow filtering and viewing the Data Field values:

Custom Field during Goods Receipt Posting in Order POD

For Order POD Goods Receipt plugin, in both POD 1.0 and POD 2.0, you can define a Custom Field as shown above, that will be displayed during goods receipt posting and can be viewed when clicking on 'Postings' button, within Goods Receipt plugin itself, or retrieved via /quantityConfirmation/v1/postings API.

Setup in POD Designer (for POD 1.0):

Setup in Manage PODs 2.0 (for POD 2.0):

Results in POD 1.0

Results in POD 2.0

Besides 'Postings', the Goods Receipt Custom Field value can also be retrieved via /quantityConfirmation/v1/postings API.

Keep in mind that, the Data to Collect on Floor Stock Receipt strategy shown above is related to Inventory IDs, and the Order POD Goods Receipt Custom Field strategy to Goods Receipt postings, and they are not connected to each other. However, out of the box capabilities like Automatic Triggers, Production Processes and API calls, together with POD custom developments or custom extensions can be evaluated to further enhance these capabilities and their impact on the overall traceability strategy.

Conclusion

Data Fields and Data Types provide a flexible foundation for extending standard SAP Digital Manufacturing processes with customer-specific traceability information. As demonstrated in this blog, they can be leveraged to enrich Inventory IDs with additional attributes that support inventory management and floor stock traceability. In addition, a Custom Field can be used to capture additional contextual information during Goods Receipt posting in Order POD.

While these capabilities address different business objects and are maintained independently, they open interesting possibilities for creating more comprehensive traceability strategies across manufacturing operations. By combining standard capabilities such as Production Processes, Automatic Triggers, APIs, and custom extensions, organizations can further automate the collection, synchronization, and collection of critical manufacturing and traceability data.

Understanding and leveraging these lesser-known configuration options can help improve visibility, reporting, compliance, and operational decision-making, while maximizing the value of the traceability data captured throughout the production lifecycle.

Do you want to see more content like this? Please live a Kudos!

If you have any questions, please check SAP Community Q&A Area, or comment down below.

Thanks,
Manoel Costa

entry.title: Re: SAP DM - run automation sequence via Postman
entry.content: Hi, you must replace the string <host> in the URL by the hostname of the Windows server your production connector is installed on. Br, Manoel

entry.title: Building a Procurement Agent for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition with SAP Joule Studio – Part 2
entry.content:
TL;DR: This is the second post of a three-part series on building a custom AI agent for Joule, specialized in creating purchase requisitions in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition through a single prompt. In this second part, we walk through the steps to build Joule Skills and a technique for item parsing using SAP Build Process Automation. Part 1 can be found here.

 

 

Understanding the Joule Skill

A Joule Skill is a modular capability that SAP Joule can invoke to execute a specific task on your behalf. It is a packaged “action” that defines:

  • Which business intent it addresses (e.g., “get allocation runs”, “list projects”);
  • Which data and systems it needs to call (APIs, services);
  • What the expected inputs and outputs are;
  • And how to execute with proper security and governance.

With Skills, in addition to answering questions, Joule can execute tasks across enterprise systems in a predictable and controlled manner.

 

Typical Structure of a Joule Skill

Here are some common elements in the structure of a Joule Skill built in Joule Studio:

  • Intent and description: the business outcome being delivered.
  • Input parameters: what Joule needs to receive (e.g., period, project ID). Skills primarily work with text (string) values for input parameters.
  • Backend action: integration and queries to APIs of SAP or external systems (through Actions).
  • Output schema: the format of the result to be returned to the user or to the next steps.

 

Joule Skill vs. Joule Agent

Compared to classical developments, Joule Skills can be seen as encapsulated functions with deterministic behavior. What does this mean? Given the same input, the expected outcome of executing a Joule Skill will always be the same.

Unlike Joule Agents, which include a reasoning step, Joule Skills follow a defined logical path, with predictable behavior and consistent results when the same input data is provided. They are tools defined through a low-code platform that will be consumed by AI agents.

Joule Agents, by contrast, are flexible and variable. They run on top of an LLM (Large Language Model) and do not follow a fixed logical path. They use AI reasoning to decide what to do, based on the context available to them. They can plan, reason, and dynamically solve problems using the tools at their disposal. They are defined through the system prompt, which will be covered in more detail in the final part of this series.

 

The Importance of Descriptions

Often, when developing a custom object in the ABAP environment, descriptions are an afterthought. How many times have you come across a structure in the data dictionary with technical field names that could not be inferred, and incomplete or barely meaningful descriptions?

When developing Joule Skills and Agents, this changes: descriptions matter. They help the agent, during the reasoning phase, to better understand what they are working with. As a result, detailed descriptions are highly welcome – both for the people who will maintain your artifacts in the future and to help the Agent achieve a higher success rate during execution.
Important: Use precise, clear English descriptions, since user requests – even when expressed in another language – are translated internally into English.

 

Procurement Scenario Considerations

Now that you understand what differentiates a Joule Agent from a Joule Skill, let’s briefly discuss how our building blocks will relate to each other and the call flow between them. The simplified diagram below illustrates the flow:

Simplified message flow through components

 

 

In the proposed scenario, when sending a single prompt, the business user will already provide the items and their respective fields. The Joule Agent will receive this prompt and, based on its definitions, split the information between the header and items, then invoke our Joule Skill to request the creation of the purchase requisition.

As mentioned, Joule Skill works with input parameters in text format. Inside it, however, the received text must be transformed into a list using an item-parsing technique. There are several ways to do this. In our case, we used a Process built with SAP Build Process Automation.

In this automation, the available script tasks were used to parse the items, to call the Products API, and subsequently to map the values back to the ISO unit of measure required by the Purchase Requisition creation API.

The items, now mapped and enriched with their corresponding units of measure, are returned to the Joule Skill, which is then ready to call our Action to create the purchase requisition. After the SAP S/4HANA execution, the PR object is returned and captured by the Joule Skill. Finally, the flow returns the ID of the generated purchase requisition.

The Joule Agent can then capture this information and present it to the business user as a message.

 

Creating a Process for Item Parsing

Roles: developers.

Although they are not strictly objects created by Joule Studio, both Processes and Automations are part of the SAP Build suite and can be reused inside Joule Skills. This allows previously designed flows and automations to also be integrated with Joule’s extended capabilities.

In our example, we need a Process for one specific reason: the purchase requisition creation Action expects items in a tabular format. Therefore, the string carrying the PR item information must be converted into rows. We will use a script task to demonstrate this case.

For didactic purposes, we will also use the Process to incorporate information from the products API and assign the corresponding ISO unit of measure. This information will also be obtained through a script.

 

To create a Process Automation project, access the SAP Build Lobby. Then, click Create. Select Automated Process and then Process. Add a name and a description for your project, review it, and confirm the creation.

Creating a Process Automation project

 

First, we configure the Trigger. This Process will be invoked from a Joule Skill, so it must work via an API call. Add a name and a description.

Adding a process trigger

 

For the case of Purchase Requisition creation, we will need a data type containing all the fields we expect to send to our Action. To create this structure, return to the newly created project and choose Create > Data Type, then provide the name and the description.

Creating a data type

 

In our use case, we created a flat structure similar to the one in the API. The fields included in the structure were:

Name

Type

List

Required

Plant

String

No

Yes

BaseUnit

String

No

No

Material

String

No

Yes

DeliveryDate

Date

No

Yes

RequestedQuantity

String

No

Yes

PurchaseRequisitionItem

String

No

Yes

PurchaseRequisition

String

No

Yes

ZJoule

String

No

No

Note: Remember that the ZJoule field was enabled through key user extensibility. If you are reproducing this example, keep in mind that this field will not exist unless you enable it beforehand. Execution will not be impacted if you have not done so – simply skip this field in all the steps.

 

Back to the Process, we will define the input/output parameters and the custom variables. Process Inputs and Outputs are the parameters received and returned by the Process. Custom variables are variables that exist only during the execution of the automation.

To access this section, click on an empty area and then on the << button.

Editing Process parameters

 

For our scenario, we defined the parameters below:

Parameter type

Name

Description

Type

Required

List

Process Input

Purchase Requisition Items – In

Purchase requisition items – trigger

String

Yes

No

Process Output

Purchase Requisition Items

Parsed purchase requisition items

Purchase Requisition Item

Yes

Yes

Custom Variable

Purchase requisition items

Purchase requisitions items

Purchase Requisition Item

N/A

Yes

Custom Variable

Product filter

Product filter string

String

N/A

No

A few additional comments:

  • “Purchase Requisition Items – In” is the parameter that contains all the items inferred by the Agent, in string format.
  • “Purchase Requisition Items” is the output parameter that will hold the items in list format. Note that we activated the List property for this parameter.
  • “Purchase requisition items” and “Product filter” are, respectively, the variable in which the items will be processed and enriched, and the variable that holds a filter string in the format expected by the OData service.

 

Now, we will start building the process flow. After the trigger, click the + icon to add a step and select the Script Task option. Here, we set the name (“Map purchase requisition items”) and click the Edit Script button to open the editor. Then we insert the JavaScript code below, which performs the item mapping and builds the product filter:

// Purchase requisition items
// Parsing items (string to JSON)
var lines = JSON.parse($.context.startEvent.purchaseRequisitionItemsIn);
var index = 0;

// Function formatDate – receives a date object and returns a string with format “yyyy-mm-dd”
function formatDate(date) {
    var yyyy = date.getFullYear();
    var mm = String(date.getMonth() + 1);
    var dd = String(date.getDate());
    if (mm.length < 2) mm = '0' + mm;
    if (dd.length < 2) dd = '0' + dd;
    return yyyy + '-' + mm + '-' + dd;
}

// From parsed array, map values to the custom variable purchaseRequisitionItems
$.context.custom.purchaseRequisitionItems = lines.map(function(line) {
    index += 10;

    return {
        PurchaseRequisition: "",
        PurchaseRequisitionItem: index.toString(),
        Material: line.Material,
        Plant: line.Plant,
        DeliveryDate: formatDate(new Date(line.DeliveryDate)),
        RequestedQuantity: parseFloat(line.RequestedQuantity).toString(),
        BaseUnit: line.BaseUnit,
        ZJoule: line.ZJoule
    };
});

// Build filter string and set value to variable productFilter
function buildFilterString(lines) {
      var filter = "Product eq '" + lines[0].Material + "'";
      for (var i = 1; i < lines.length; i++) {
          filter += " or Product eq '" + lines[i].Material + "'";
      }
      return filter;
}

$.context.custom.productFilter = buildFilterString(lines);

 Important: The JavaScript code provided must be compatible with ECMAScript 5.1.

 

Creating a script task

Note: If you are not familiar with creating scripts, refer to the page Create and Configure a Script Task

Next, the API to retrieve product/material details will be called. We pick the Action for products created in part 1. When you select it, a new dependency will be added to your project.

Adding an Action to your Process flow

 

 

You will need to create a destination variable (which will be requested when testing and deploying this Process) and the input parameters. We assigned the variable Product filter as the value mapped to the $filter parameter.

 

Then we add a second script that looks up the result in the product list and maps the ISO unit of measure to the Purchase Requisition Items variable.

  var sourceList = $.context.action_get_Product_1.result.value;
  var targetList = $.context.custom.purchaseRequisitionItems;

  // Build a lookup map from Product -> BaseISOUnit
  var sourceMap = {};
  for (var i = 0; i < sourceList.length; i++) {
      var sourceItem = sourceList[i];
      sourceMap[sourceItem.Product] = sourceItem.BaseISOUnit;
  }

  // Map BaseISOUnit to BaseUnit in the target list
  for (var j = 0; j < targetList.length; j++) {
      var targetItem = targetList[j];
      var material = targetItem.Material;
      if (sourceMap.hasOwnProperty(material)) {
          targetItem.BaseUnit = sourceMap[material];
      }
  }

 

Adding the second script task

 

The last step in building the Process is mapping its output. When you click End, the side menu displays the Process Outputs section. Here, the value of the Purchase Requisition Items variable will be transferred to the result. Since both structures are of the same type, all fields will be automatically filled in.

Mapping the Process output

 

The Process is now ready to be saved, released, and deployed to the Easy Purchase Requisition environment. This sequence can be executed by clicking Save and then Release. A pop-up will open, where you can enter the version details. Once released, you can click the generated version and select Deploy, then choose your environment and the destination used internally by the API (in our example, EPRProductV4).

 

Saving, releasing, and deploying a Process

 

Back at the lobby, you must publish your automation. If you do not, it will not be available to your Joule Skill. To publish, select the Process, navigate to the Versions tab, click the “…” button on your Released version, and choose the option Publish to Library.

Publishing a Process

 

Creating a Joule Skill

Roles: functional consultants and developers.

The item-parsing prerequisites have been met by the automation we just generated. Now we can proceed with the creation of a Joule Skill.

First, we go to the project. From the SAP Build Lobby, choose Create > Joule Agent and Skill.

Creating a Joule Agent and Skill project from SAP Build lobby

 

Provide a name and a description for your project.

Project name and description

If you need additional information on how to proceed with project creation, refer to the official documentation.

 

When the project opens, select Create > Joule Skill.

Creating a Joule Skill

 

In the pop-up, give your new capability a meaningful name and a detailed description of the purpose it covers. Remember: descriptions help the agent identify the most relevant case for a request from your business user. You should include information about the goal and the parameters expected for its execution.

Joule Skill - initial parameters

 

For our use case, we set the name to “Create a Purchase Requisition” and the description to “A skill to create any type of purchase requisition, based on the PR type and items described through a single prompt, including custom fields (e.g. ZJoule)”.

 

Structuring a Joule Skill is very similar to structuring a Process or an Automation in SAP Build. You will have access to a low-code tool to structure the process flow. To learn everything you can do in a Joule Skill, refer to the documentation Create a Joule Skill.

 

First, we need to define the behavior of the Joule Skill. By clicking anywhere on the background and then on the << button, the configurations will be displayed. In the General tab, you will find the name and description proposed earlier. If necessary, you can also correct or enhance them. There are also two switches:

  • Allow Joule to generate a response: when enabled, this option allows Joule to extract information from the skill and turn it into a response. If you decide not to enable it, you will need to configure the “Send Message” steps to deliver the response. For more information on managing responses, refer to “Send a Response”.
  • Allow skill to be started directly by a user: when enabled, this option allows the capability to be invoked directly by the user, i.e., without an agent or another capability acting as an intermediary. When this option is disabled, the Joule Skill must necessarily have at least one output parameter.

General tab - Joule Skill

 

For this use case, we used:

  • Allow Joule to generate a response: enabled. This will allow Joule to capture the capability’s return and compose its own response.
  • Allow skill to be started directly by a user: disabled. Since there is a step that captures and stringifies the items with a specific structure, it is not advisable to allow the user to invoke the skill directly. We will delegate to the Joule Agent the task of formatting the parameters and invoking the capability.

 

On the Parameters tab, just as we did for the Process, we define the input and output parameters and variables.

Setting input and output parameters

 

In our procurement example, the parameters were configured according to the table below:

Parameter type

Name

Description

Type

Required

List

Skill Input

Purchase Requisition Items

Purchase requisition items as a stringified array

String

Yes

No

Skill Input

Purchase Requisition Type

Purchase requisition type

String

Yes

No

Skill Input

Purchase Requisition Description

Purchase requisition description

String

No

No

Skill Input

Purchase Requisition Header Note

Purchase requisition header note

String

No

No

Skill Output

Purchase Requisition ID

Generated purchase requisition ID

String

No

No

Skill Variable

Purchase Requisition ID

Purchase requisition ID

String

N/A

Yes

 

After completing the parameter configuration, we will create the flow to be executed. To do so, click the + symbol and insert the steps, mapping the inputs and outputs of each activity type.

 Adding a Process task to Joule Skill

 

In our example, we started by inserting the Process Easy Purchase Requisition – Item Parsing in order to transform and enrich the items described by the input parameter. We requested that execution be awaited until completion so that the process could return its output, containing a list of items with their respective ISO units of measure mapped.

The table below summarizes the configuration applied to this step:

Parameter type

Name

Value

General

Step Name

Map Items

General

Wait for Output Result

true

Input

Purchase Requisition Items – In

Skill Inputs > Purchase requisition items

 

Adding an Action call

 

Mapping Action parameters

 

Next, we inserted the Action (API) call to create a purchase requisition. Similar to what was done in the Process for the products Action, here we mapped the inputs and outputs. We also defined an environment variable that holds the destination name to be used by the API call.

The following table describes this mapping:

Parameter type

Name

Value

General

Step Name

Create a purchase requisition

General

Destination Value

DESTINATION_PR

Input

_PurchaseRequisitionItem (Bind List)

Map items > list - Purchase Requisition Items

Input

_PurchaseRequisitionItem > BaseUnitISOCode

Map items > list - Purchase Requisition Items > BaseUnit

Input

_PurchaseRequisitionItem > DeliveryDate

Map items > list - Purchase Requisition Items > DeliveryDate

Input

_PurchaseRequisitionItem > Material

Map items > list - Purchase Requisition Items > Material

Input

_PurchaseRequisitionItem > Plant

Map items > list - Purchase Requisition Items > Plant

Input

_PurchaseRequisitionItem > PurchaseRequisition

Map items > list - Purchase Requisition Items > PurchaseRequisition

Input

_PurchaseRequisitionItem > PurchaseRequisitionItem

Map items > list - Purchase Requisition Items > PurchaseRequisitionItem

Input

_PurchaseRequisitionItem > RequestedQuantity

Map items > list - Purchase Requisition Items > RequestedQuantity

Input

_PurchaseRequisitionItem > ZJoule

Map items > list - Purchase Requisition Items > ZZ1_ZJoule_PRI

Input

PurchaseRequisition

Custom variables > Purchase requisition ID

Input

PurchaseRequisitionType

Skill Inputs > Purchase requisition type

Input

PurchaseReqnDescription

Skill Inputs > Purchase requisition description

Input

PurReqnHeaderNote

Skill Inputs > Purchase requisition header note

 

Setting variables and Skill output

 

The last step assigns the resulting purchase requisition ID value, generated through the API call, to a local variable using the Set Variables activity.

Section

Name

Value

General

Step Name

Set purchase requisition ID

General > Set Custom Variable

Purchase requisition ID

Create a purchase requisition > result > PurchaseRequisition

 

Then we set the value of this same variable as our Skill Output.

Mapping the Skill output

 

Click Save to store your Joule Skill. You don’t need to release or deploy it right now, since we are still creating the Joule Agent in part 3.

 

Key Takeaways

If you have made it this far and followed the example, you now know how to:

  • Identify the structure of a Joule Skill.
  • Distinguish a Joule Skill from a Joule Agent (deterministic vs. reasoning).
  • Create a Process or an Automation and use these objects to support item parsing.
  • Create a project for Joule Skills and Agents and start building a Joule Skill.
  • Consume Actions and build a logical flow for your custom capability, mapping inputs and outputs.

Always remember the importance of properly describing the capability and its parameters to facilitate the agent’s inference process.

 

Next Steps

In the next and final post in this series, we will cover the creation of the Joule Agent. There, we will use the artifacts from this guide to enable the Joule Agent to perform the desired operation. We will also walk through the process of making your agent available, allowing it to coexist with the standard Joule capabilities in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. Thanks for reading, and see you there!

 

Thanks,
Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG Team
